GroupTheory.standard notations for representing the: The two standard notations for representing the integers mod n are \mathbb{Z}_n and \mathbb{Z}(n\mathbb{Z}).
GroupTheory.the standard notation for representing t: The standard notation for representing the integers mod n, relatively prime to n is \mathbb{Z}<^x_n.
GroupTheory.notation for representing the dihedral g: The book's notation for dihedral groups is D_{2n}.
GroupTheory.the Dihedral group?: The dihedral group is the set of symmetries of a regular n-gon.     {a ^n = 1, b ^2 = 1, ab = ba ^{-1}}
GroupTheory.two standard notations for representing: The two standard notations for the dihedral group are D_n and D_{2n}.
GroupTheory.dihedral group described by relations an: The dihedral group D_n is described by the following relation:      a ^n = 1, b ^2 = 1, ba = a ^{-1}b = a^{n-1}b.
GroupTheory.a given set of relations describes a gr: One would prove that a finite group, G, is described by a set of relations by showing that any string can be reduced to at most |G|.
GroupTheory.the group of permutations of n objects?: S_n is the group of permutations on n objects.
GroupTheory.the group of invertible matrices?: GL_n(R) is the group of invertible n X n matrices, where each element of the matrix belongs to R.
GroupTheory.the group GL_n(X)?: GL_n(R) is the group of invertible n X n matrices, where each element of the matrix belongs to R.
GroupTheory.the group S_n?: S_n is the group of permutations on n objects.
GroupTheory.the group D_n?: D_n is the set of symmetries of a regular n-gon.     {a ^n = 1, b ^2 = 1, ab = ba ^{-1}}
GroupTheory.the group D_{2n}?: D_{2n} is the set of symmetries of a regular n-gon.     {a ^n = 1, b ^2 = 1, ab = ba ^{-1}}
GroupTheory.the group \\mathbb{Z}_n?: \mathbb{Z}_n is the set of integers mod n.
GroupTheory.the group \\mathbb{Z}/(n\\mathbb{Z})?: The group \mathbb{Z}/(n\mathbb{Z}) is the set of integers mod n?
GroupTheory.the group \\mathbb{Z}_n^x?: \mathbb{Z}^x _n is the set of integers mod n relatively prime to n.
GroupTheory.the order of an element in a group?: The order of an element, g, is the smallest positive element such that g^k = 1.
GroupTheory.the order of an element in a group and t: The order of an element of a group always divides the order of the group.
GroupTheory.the result of raising an element of a gr: For an element, g, of a group, G, g^{|G|k} = 1.
GroupTheory.\\lta\\gt?: If G is a group and a \in G, write \lta\gt = {a ^n : n \in \mathbb{Z}} = {all powers of a}.
GroupTheory.the cyclic group generated by a?: \lta\gt is called the cyclic group generated by a = {a ^n : n \in \mathbb{Z}} = {all powers of a}.
GroupTheory.finite cyclic groups and smaller finite: All subgroups of a cyclic group are cyclic.
GroupTheory.the intersection of subgroups of G and G: The intersection of any family of subgroups of a group G is a subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.\\ltX\\gt, where X is a subset of G?: If X is a subset of G, then \ltX\gt is called the subgroup generated by X.
GroupTheory.p being prime say about \\mathbb{Z}_p^x?: If p is prime then \mathbb{Z}^x_p is cyclic.
GroupTheory.a primitive root?: Any generator of G is called a primitive root of G.
GroupTheory.elements of a cyclic group that can gene: If G = \ltg\gt, then the generators of G are the elements of the form g^k with gcd(k, n) = 1
GroupTheory.subgroups of a cyclic group are there o: For every divisor of the order of a cyclic group, n, there is exactly one subgroup.
GroupTheory.subgroups of cyclic groups?: All subgroups of a cyclic groups are cyclic.
GroupTheory.Euler's \\phi function state?: Euler's \phi(n) function returns the number of integers, 1 \le i \le n that are relatively prime to n.
GroupTheory.a permutation of n objects?: A permutation of n objects is a one-to-one function from {1, 2, ..., n} to {1, 2, ..., n}.
GroupTheory.multiplication of permutations?: Multiplying permutations is done by composition of functions from right to left.
GroupTheory.a transposition?: A transposition is a 2-cycle in S_n.
GroupTheory.permutations written as cycles?: Permutations are written as cycles by recording each element maps to in the function.
GroupTheory.tell the order of a permutation from the: If \sigma is a product of disjoint cycles of lengths m_1, m_2, ..., m_r then the order of \sigma is the lcm (m_1, m_2, ..., m_r).
GroupTheory.cycles and 2-cycles?: Every cycle can be represented as a product of 2-cycles.
GroupTheory.cycles be represented as 2-cycles?: Start with the first member of the cycle and map it to every other member of the cycle from last to first.
GroupTheory.permutation to be odd?: A permutation is odd if it can be written as a product of an even number of 2-cycles.
GroupTheory.permutation to be even?: A permutation is even if it can be written as a product of an odd number of 2-cycles.
GroupTheory.S_n?: The order of S_n is n!.
GroupTheory.A_n?: The order of A_n is n!/2.
GroupTheory.a left coset?: If g is an element of G and H is a subgroup of G, then gH is a left coset.
GroupTheory.a right coset?: If g is an element of G and H is a subgroup of G, then Hg is a right coset.
GroupTheory.intersection of two cosets being nonempt: If aH \cap bH \ne \emptyset , then aH = bH.
GroupTheory.two cosets equal?: aH = bH if and only if b ^{-1}a \in H.
GroupTheory.a group and the cosets of a subgroup?: A group is the union of disjoint cosets of a subgroup.
GroupTheory.equivalence relations and groups?: A subgroup forms an equivalence relation on a group, a ~ b if ab ^{-1} \in H.
GroupTheory.the order of a subgroup and the order of: The order of a subgroup is equal to the order of a coset of that subgroup.
GroupTheory.Lagrange's Theorem say?: Lagrange's Theorem says that the order of a subgroup of a group divides the order of the group.
GroupTheory.the order of a subgroup and the order of: Lagrange's Theorem says that the order of a subgroup of a group divides the order of the group.
GroupTheory.the order of an element of group and the: The order of an element will divide the order of the group.
GroupTheory.the index of a subgroup?: The index of a subgroup measures the number of cosets of that subgroup.
GroupTheory.the number of cosets of a subgroup?: One would know the number of cosets of a subgroup by calculating the index of the subgroup.
GroupTheory.[G : H]?: [G : H] represents the index of the subgroup.
GroupTheory.one know about a group of prime order?: If p is prime then every group of order p is cyclic.
GroupTheory.Euler's Theorem say?: Euler's Theorem says that if gcd (r, m) = 1, then r^{ \phi (m)} \approx 1 mod m.
GroupTheory.Euler's Theorem proved?: Euler's theorem is proved by letting G = \mathbb{Z}^x_n and calculating a^{\phi(n)}.
GroupTheory.Fermat's (Little) Theorem?: Fermat's (Little) Theorem says that if p is a prime, then a^p = 1 (mod p).
GroupTheory.Wilson's Theorem say?: Wilson's Theorem says that an integer p is a prime if and only if (p - 1)! = -1 mod p.
GroupTheory.a homomorphism?: A homomorphism \phi: G_1 \to G_2 is a map satisfying \phi(ab) = \phi(a)\phi(b) for all a, b \in G_1.
GroupTheory.identity map to in a homomorphism?: In a homomorphism, the identity of G_1 always maps to the identity of G_2.
GroupTheory.a bijective homomorphism?: A bijective homomorphism is a homomorphism that is 1-1 and onto.
GroupTheory.an injective homomorphism?: An injective homomorphism is a homomorphism where no two elements map to the same element.
GroupTheory.a surjective homomorphism?: A surjective homomorphism is a homomorphism where every element in the range is mapped to.
GroupTheory.a 1-1 homomorphism?: A 1-1 homomorphism is a homomorphism where no two elements map to the same element.
GroupTheory.an onto homomorphism?: An onto homomorphism is a homomorphism where every element in the range is mapped to.
GroupTheory.it mean for two groups to be isomorphic?: Two groups are isomorphic if there exists a homomorphism between them that is both 1-1 and onto.
GroupTheory.the kernel of a homomorphism?: The kernel of \phi is defined as Ker(\phi) = {a \in G_1 | \phi(a) = 1}     So the kernel is the set of elements of G_1 that map to the identity of G_2.
GroupTheory.the notation for the kernel of a homomor: The notation for the kernel of \phi is Ker (\phi).
GroupTheory.the image of a homomorphism?: The image of \phi is defined as Im (\phi) = {x \in G_2 | \thereexists a \in G_1 such that \phi(a) = x}So basically the image of a homomorphism is the set of elements of G_2 that are mapped to by \phi .
GroupTheory.the notation for the image of a homomorp: The notation for the image of \phi is Im (\phi).
GroupTheory.the kernel of a homomorphism and the dom: The kernel of a homomorphism is a normal subgroup of its domain.
GroupTheory.the image of a homomorphism and the rang: The image of a homomorphism is a subgroup of the range.
GroupTheory.an element belonging to the kernel of a: If x \in Ker \phi and if a \in G, then axa ^{-1} \in Ker \phi .
GroupTheory.How does the kernel of a homomorphism re: \phi is injective if and only if Ker(\phi) = {1}.
GroupTheory.one use kernels to prove injection?: If the kernel of the homomorphism is {1}, then we know its an injection and vice-versa.
GroupTheory.a conjugate of x?: A conjugate of x is any element of the form gxg ^{-1}, where g \in G.
GroupTheory.conjugation by g?: A conjugation by g is the mapping \gamma_g(a) = gag ^{-1}.
GroupTheory.a conjugate of an element of G?: Conjugate elements have the same order.
GroupTheory.an automorphism?: An isomorphism from G \to G is called an automorphism.
GroupTheory.Aut(G)?: Aut(G) is the set of all automorphisms of G.
GroupTheory.the set of all automorphisms of G?: The set of all automorphisms of G is called Aut(G).
GroupTheory.an inner automorphism?: An inner automorphism is a mapping \gamma_g(a) = gag ^{-1}.
GroupTheory.the set of all inner automorphisms?: The set of all inner automorphisms is denoted Inn(G).
GroupTheory.Inn(G)?: Inn(G) represents the set of all inner automorphisms of G.
GroupTheory.Aut(G) and Z(G)?: The mapping \gamma(g) = \phi_g(x) = gxg ^{-1} gives a homomorphism with kernel Z(G)
GroupTheory.Inn(G) and Aut(G)?: Inn(G) is a normal subgroup of Aut(G).
GroupTheory.a conjugacy class?: The set x_g = {y \in G | y = axa ^{-1} for some a \in G} is called the conjugacy class of x.
GroupTheory.conjugacy class of an element describe?: The conjugacy class of an element describes the set of all elements that are conjugates of the given element.
GroupTheory.permutations and their conjugates (the c: Two permutations are conjugate in S_n if and only if they have the same cycle structure.
GroupTheory.If two permutations have the same cycle: Two permutations have the same cycle structure if and only if these two permutations are conjugate in S_n.
GroupTheory.the notation for the center of a group?: The notation for the center of a group is Z(G).
GroupTheory.the center of a group?: The set of elements of G that commute with all of G is called the center of G.
GroupTheory.the notation for isomorphic groups?: If G_1 is isomorphic to G_2, then we write G_1 \cong G_2.
GroupTheory.the product of two subgroups (XY)?: The product of the subgroups X and Y is XY = {xy : x \in X, y \in Y}.
GroupTheory.product of two subgroups (XY) a subgroup: If H and K are subgroups of G and one of them is normal, then HK is a subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.product of two subgroups (XY) normal in: If H and K are normal subgroups of G, then HK is a normal subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.a normal subgroup?: A subgroup K of a group G is called normal if gK = Kg for all g \in G.
GroupTheory.HK is a normal subgroup of G?: (1)HK is a normal subgroup of G if and only if HK = KH.     (2)if H is a normal subgroup and K is a normal subgroup, then HK is a normal subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.order of HK?: If H and K are subgroups of G, then |HK| |H \cap K| = |H| |K|.
GroupTheory.a homomorphism and normal subgroups?: The kernel of a homomorphism, where G is the domain, is a normal subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.a quotient group?: If G is a group and K is a normal subgroup of G, then the quotient group is the set of cosets of K.
GroupTheory.an element of a group and the index of a: An element raised to the index of a normal subgroup will belong to that subgroup.
GroupTheory.the first isomorphism theorem?: Let \phi:G_1 \to G_2 be a homomorphism.     Then G_1/Ker(\phi) \cong Im(\phi), where \phi(g_1Ker(\phi)) = \phi(g_1).
GroupTheory.the kernel of a homomorphism and the ima: The quotient group of the kernel of a homomorphism is isomorphic to the image of that homomorphism.
GroupTheory.the second homomorphism theorem?: Let G be a group, H be a subgroup of G, and K be normal in G. Then      (1) H \cap K is normal in H.     (2) HK is a subgroup of G.     (3) H/H\capK \cong HK/K.
GroupTheory.the intersection of a subgroup and a nor: The second isomorphism theorem says that if H and K are subgroups of a group G, with H normal in G, then H \cap K is normal in K.
GroupTheory.If H is a subgroup of G and K is a norma: If H is a subgroup of G and K is a normal subgroup of G then HK is a subgroup of G.
If H is a subgroup of G and K is a normal subgroup of G then H/H\capK is isomorphic to HK/K.
If H is a subgroup of G and K is a normal subgroup of G then HK/K is isomorphic to H/H\capK.
GroupTheory.the Third Isomorphism Theorem?: Let H, K be normal subgroups of G, with K \subseteq H. Then (G/K)/(H/K) \cong G/H.
GroupTheory.(G/K)/(H/K) isomorphic to, where K \\sub: By the third isomorphism theorem, (G/K)/(H/K) is isomorphic to G/H.
GroupTheory.If H and K are normal subgroups of G, wh: Let H, K be normal subgroups of G. Suppose that H \cap K = {1}.Then hk = kh for all h \in H and k \in K.
GroupTheory.normal subgroups and conjugacy classes?: Every normal subgroup of a group is a union of conjugacy classes of G.
GroupTheory.maximal normal subgroup and its quotient: The quotient group of a maximal normal subgroup is simple.
GroupTheory.a simple quotient group, G / H and H?: If G / H is simple, then H is a maximal normal subgroup.
GroupTheory.maximal normal subgroups and simple grou: A normal subgroup H is a maximal subgroup of G if and only if G / H is a simple group.
GroupTheory.G has an element of a certain order?: If G is a finite group and p is a prime divisor of G, then G has an element of order p.
GroupTheory.the quotient of Z(G) and G being abelian: If G is a group and G / Z(G) is cyclic the G is abelian.
GroupTheory.can we say about a group with at most on: If G is a group of order n having at most one cyclic subgroup of order d for each divisor d of n, then G is cyclic.
GroupTheory.If |a| = m and |b| = n, where gcd(m, n): If a, b \in G and ab = ba, then |ab| = |a| |b|.
GroupTheory.a Hamiltonian group?: A group is called Hamiltonian if every subgroup is normal. Every Hamiltonian group has the form Q \times A, where A is an abelian group with no elements of order 4.
GroupTheory.Hamiltonian groups and the quaternian gr: Every Hamiltonian group has the form Q \times A, where Q is the quaternian group and A is an abelian group with no elements of order 4.
GroupTheory.HK \\cong H \\times K?: If H and K are normal subgroups of G, and H \cap K = {1}, and HK = G, then G \cong H \times K.
GroupTheory.the direct product of two groups?: The direct product of two groups is the set of all ordered pairs (h, k) with h \in H and k \in K.
GroupTheory.the notation for the direct product of t: The notation for the direct product is H \times K.
GroupTheory.the direct product of two cyclic groups: The direct product of two cyclic groups of relatively prime order is isomorphic to the cyclic group whose order is the product of their orders.      \mathbb{Z}_{mn} \cong \mathbb{Z}_m \times \mathbb{Z}_n.
GroupTheory.a*b if |a| = m and |b| = n, ab=ba and gc: if |a| = m and |b| = n, ab=ba and gcd(m, n) = 1, then |ab| = m*n.
GroupTheory.If G is cyclic, and d divides |G|, subg: A cyclic group has a unique subgroup of order d for each divisor of its order.
GroupTheory.a cyclic group and the number of subgrou: A group is cyclic if and only if it has at most one subgroup for each divisor of its order.
GroupTheory.the commutator subgroup?: The subgroup generated by {xyx ^{-1}y ^{-1} | xy \in G} (smallest subgroup containing these elements) is called the commutator subgroup, G_c.
GroupTheory.the commutator subgroup of an abelian gr: The commutator subgroup of an abelian group is G_c = {1}.
GroupTheory.G if G_c = {1}?: When G_c = {1}, we know that G is abelian.
GroupTheory.G_c?: G_c represents the commutator subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.the abelianization of G?: the quotient group of G/G_c is called the abelianization of G.
GroupTheory.G/G_c?: the quotient group of G/G_c is called the abelianization of G.
GroupTheory.Caley's theorem say?: If |G| = n, then G is isomorphic to a subgroup of S_n.
GroupTheory.a group and S_n?: If |G| = n, then G is isomorphic to a subgroup of S_n.
GroupTheory.representation by cosets say?: If H is a subgroup of G with index n, there exists a homomorphism \phi : G \to S_n, with Ker \phi \subseteq H.
GroupTheory.a subgroup of G and S_n?: If H is a subgroup of G with index n, there exists a homomorphism \phi : G \to S_n, with Ker \phi \subseteq H.
GroupTheory.a group G to acting on a set X?: If G is a group and X is a set, then G acts on X if there is a function from G \times X \to X, denoted by (g, x) \mapsto gx, such that      1. (gh)x = g(hx) for all g, h \in G and x \in X;      2. 1x = x for all x \in X where 1 is the identity in G.
GroupTheory.a G-set?: X is a G-set when X is being acted on by a group G.
GroupTheory.the orbit of an element of a G-set?: The orbit of an element of X is the set {gx | g \in G}.
GroupTheory.the stabilizer of an element of an eleme: The stabilizer of an element of X is the set {g \in G | gx = x}.
GroupTheory.a group to acting transitively on a set?: G acts transitively on a set if there is only one orbit.
GroupTheory.the centralizer of an element of a group: The centralizer of an element of a group is the set {g \in G | gxg ^{-1} = x}.
GroupTheory.Is the centralizer of an element a subgr: The centralizer of an element of a group is a subgroup of that group.
GroupTheory.a conjugate of a subgroup?: If H is a subgroup of G, a conjugate of H is a subgroup of the form aHa ^{-1} = {aHa ^{-1} | h \in H}, where a \in G.
GroupTheory.a normalizer of a subgroup?: If H is a subgroup of G, the normalizer of H is the subgroup of the form N_g(H) = {g \in G | ghg ^{-1} = H}.
GroupTheory.Is the conjugate of a subgroup a group?: The conjugates of a subgroup of G are themselves subgroups of G.
GroupTheory.Is the normalizer of a subgroup a group?: The normalizer of a subgroup of G is a subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.the class equation?: The class equation of a finite group G is      |G| = |Z(G)| + \sum _i[G : C_g(x_i)].
GroupTheory.the orbits of the elements of a finite G: If X is a G-set, then X is a disjoint union of the orbits of its elements.
GroupTheory.the order of a G-set and the order of it: The order of a G-set is equal to the sum of its orbits.
GroupTheory.the order of the orbit of an element of: The order of the orbit is the index of the stabilizer, |O(x)| = [G : G_x] .
GroupTheory.the number of conjugates of an element a: The order of its conjugacy class (number of conjugates) is the index of its centralizer, |x^G| = [G : C_g(x)] .
GroupTheory.a p-group?: A p-group is a group of the order p ^n
GroupTheory.If G is a p-group, then the center of G?: If p is a prime and G is a p-group, then |Z(G)| \ne {1}.
GroupTheory.groups of order p ^2 and abelian groups?: If G is a group of order p ^2 then G is abelian.
GroupTheory.groups of order p^e and normal subgroups: If G is a group of order p^e then G has a normal subgroup of order p^k for every k less than e.
GroupTheory.a simple group?: A group G is called simple if it has no normal subgroups other than {1} and itself.
GroupTheory.an abelian group simple?: An abelian group is simple if and only if it is finite and of prime order.
GroupTheory.groups of prime order and simplicity?: If a group is finite and of prime order, then it is abelian and simple.
GroupTheory.a normal subgroup of A_5?: A_5, {1} are the only normal subgroup of A_5
GroupTheory.a normal subgroup of A_6?: A_6, {1} are the only normal subgroup of A_6
GroupTheory.a normal subgroup of A_n?: A_n, {1} are is the only normal subgroup of A_n
GroupTheory.a Sylow p-subgroup?: A Sylow p-subgroup is a subgroup, P, of G where |P| = p^e and p^{e+1} does not divide |G|.
GroupTheory.a maximal p-subgroup?: A maximal P-subgroup is a subgroup that is not contained in any other P-Subgroups.
GroupTheory.groups and Sylow subgroups?: If p is a prime such that p divides |G|, then a Sylow p-subgroup exists.
GroupTheory.p-groups and Sylow subgroups?: Given a group G, every p-subgroup of G is contained in some Sylow p-subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.Sylow p-groups and its conjugates?: Every conjugate of a Sylow p-subgroup is also a Sylow p-subgroup of G.     And every Sylow p-subgroup of G is a conjugate of every other Sylow p-subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.Sylow p-subgroups of a group G are ther: The number of Sylow p-subgroups, n, of G is of the form n \approx 1 (mod p).       and n is a divisor of m, where |G| = p^em.
GroupTheory.N_g(P) and the number of Sylow subgroups: The number of Sylow subgroups is the index in G of the normalizer N_g(P).
GroupTheory.If |a| = p^m and aPa ^{-1} = P, then a?: If a \in G has order some power of p and if aPa ^{-1} = P, then a \in P.
GroupTheory.If there are r Sylow p_j-subgroups of a: If |G| = p^em, and there are r Sylow p-subgroups, then r \approx 1 (mod p) and r is a divisor of |G| / p^e = m.
GroupTheory.finite group have a unique Sylow p-subgr: A finite group G has a unique Sylow p-subgroup, P, for some prime p, if and only if P is normal in G.
GroupTheory.a Sylow p-subgroup being normal imply ab: A finite group G has a unique Sylow p-subgroup, for some prime p, if and only if P is normal in G.
GroupTheory.If |G| = p^em, then each of G's Sylow p-: If G is a finite group of order p^em, where p is a prime and p \notdiv m, then every Sylow p-subgroup P of G has order p^e
GroupTheory.a nilpotent group?: A nilpotent group is a group that is the direct product of its Sylow subgroups.
GroupTheory.G, when all of the Sylow subgroups of G: A finite group G all of whose Sylow subgroups are normal is the direct product of its Sylow subgroups.
GroupTheory.subgroup is normal?: The different ways of showing a subgroup is normal are:      1) gK = Kg for all g \in G.     2)gKg ^{-1} = K for all g \in G.      3)gKg ^{-1} \subseteq K for all g \in G.      4)Show that K is the kernel of a homomorphism.     5) If its a Sylow subgroup, show that there is only one Sylow of that order.      6) Show that it is a subgroup that is the union of conjugacy classes.      7) Show that H has index 2
GroupTheory.a group is abelian?: The different ways of showing a group is abelian are:      1. Show that hg = gh for all g, h \in G.      2. Show that Z(G) = G.      3. Show that G_c = G.
GroupTheory.subgroups are always normal in G?: The subgroups that are always normal in G are:      1. Z(G) is always normal in G.      2. The kernel of any homomorphism whose domain is G is normal in G.
GroupTheory.|x| and |x ^{-1}|?: For an element, x, of G x and x ^{-1} have the same order.
GroupTheory.A \\times B abelian?: A \times B is abelian if and only if A and B are abelian.
GroupTheory.can we infer from A \\times B being abel: A \times B is abelian if and only if A and B are abelian.
GroupTheory.(a, b) in A \\times B?: The order of (a, b) is the least common multiple of |a| and |b|.
GroupTheory.Let |x| = n, where n is odd. x^i = x^{-i: Let |x| = n. If n is odd, then x^i \ne x ^{-1} for all i \in {1, 2, ..., n-1}
GroupTheory.Let |x| = n, where n is even. x^i = x ^{: Let |x| = n, where n = 2k, then x^i = x ^{-1} if and only if i = k
GroupTheory.If \\sigma is an m-cycle, \\sigma^i an m: If \sigma is an m-cycle, then \sigma^i is an m-cycle if and only if i is relatively prime to m.
GroupTheory.\\phi(g) = g ^{-1} a homomorphism?: Let G be any group. \phi(g) = g ^{-1} is a homomorphism if and only if G is abelian.
GroupTheory.\\phi(g) = g ^2 a homomorphism?: Let G be any group. \phi(g) = g ^2 is a homomorphism if and only if G is abelian.
GroupTheory.can we infer from an automorphism that f: Let G be a finite group possesses an automorphism \sigma such that \sigma(g) = g if and only if g = 1.      Then G is abelian.           If G has an automorphism that fixes only the identity and has order 2 in Aut(G), then G is abelian.
GroupTheory.C_g(Z(G))? the set of elements that: Every element of G fixes the center of G. C_g(Z(G)) = G.
GroupTheory.subsets, A \\subseteq B and the centrali: If A and B are subsets of G with A \subseteq B, then C_g(B) is a subgroup of C_g(A),
GroupTheory.Z(G) and N_g(A) for a subset A of G?: For any subset A of G, Z(G) \le N_g(A)
GroupTheory.\\lt H, Z(G) \\gt abelian?: If H is an abelian subgroup of G then &lt H, Z(G) \gt is abelian.
GroupTheory.a maximal subgroup?: A subgroup M of a group is called a maximal subgroup if M \ne G and the only subgroups of G contain M are M and G.
GroupTheory.cyclic groups and maximal subgroups?: If G = \lt x \gt is a cyclic group of order n \ge 1 then a subgroup H is maximal if and only if H = \lt x^p \gt for some prime p dividing n.
GroupTheory.finite groups and maximal subgroups?: If H is a proper subgroup of a finite group G then there is a maximal subgroup containing H.
GroupTheory.a divisible group?: A nontrivial abelian group A is called divisible if for each element a \in A and each nonzero integer k there is an element x \in A such that x^k = a
GroupTheory.the relationship finite groups and divis: No finite abelian group is divisible.
GroupTheory.If H \\le G and N \\triangleleft H, N_gH: If H \le G and N is a normal subgroup of H then H \le N_g(N).
GroupTheory.HK a subgroup of G (in reference to N_g(: If H and K are subgroups of G and H \le N_g(K), then HK is a subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.H normalizing K?: If A is any subset of N_g(K), then we say that A normalizes K.
GroupTheory.H centralizing K?: If A is any subset of C_g(K), then we say that A centralizes K.
GroupTheory.Give an example of a bijection between l: Let H \le G. The map \phi(x) = x ^{-1} gives a bijection between the set of left cosets and the set of right cosets of H in G.
GroupTheory.If G = S_n, then stabilizer of an elemen: Let G = S_n and i \in {1, 2, ..., n-1}. Then the stabilizer of i is isomorphic to S_{n-1}.
GroupTheory.Given a group G, a subgroup H, and a nor: Let G be a finite group, let H \le G and let N \triangleleft G. Then if |H| and [G : N] are relatively prime, then H \le N
GroupTheory.In a group, not necessarily cyclic, N is: If N is a normal subgroup of the finite group G and ( |N|, [G : N] ) = 1, then N is the unique subgroup of G of order |N|.
GroupTheory.Given a normal subgroup, N, with N \\le: Given subgroups A and B of G and a normal subgroup N, where N \le A and N \le B, A \le B if and only if A / N \le B / N.
GroupTheory.[B: A] and [B / N : A / N], where A \\le: Given subgroups A and B of G and a normal subgroup N, where N \le A and N \le B, if A \le B, then [B : A]  = [B / N : A / N].
GroupTheory.can we conclude from a normal subgroup h: If H is a normal subgroup of G of prime index p, then for all K \le G either      1. K \le H or      2. G = H and [ K : K \cap H ] = p
GroupTheory.elements of order 2 in A_n and S_n: Every element of order 2 in A_n is the square of an order 4 element in S_n.
GroupTheory.group is generated by an element of orde: The subgroup of A_4 generated by any element of order 2 and any element of order 3 is all of A_4.
GroupTheory.2 3-cycles of S_5 generate A_4?: Let x and y be distinct 3-cycles with x \ne y ^{-1}     1. If x and y fix a common element of {1, ..., 5}, then \lt x, y \gt \cong A_5.
GroupTheory.2 3-cycles of S_5 generate A_5?: Let x and y be distinct 3-cycles with x \ne y ^{-1}     1. If x and y do not fix a common element of {1, ..., 5}, then \lt x, y \gt \cong A_5.
GroupTheory.2 3-cycles of S_n generate \\mathbb{Z}_n: Let x and y be distinct 3-cycles     1. If x = y ^{-1} then \lt x, y \gt \cong \mathbb{Z}_3.
GroupTheory.2 3-cycles of S_n generate \\mathbb{Z}_3: Let x and y be distinct 3-cycles of S_6     1. If x and y commute, then \lt x, y \gt \cong \mathbb{Z}_3 \times \mathbb{Z}_3
GroupTheory.normal subgroups and primes dividing the: If G is a finite group of order n and p is the smallest prime dividing |G|, then any subgroup of index p is normal.
GroupTheory.the maximum size for a given conjugacy c: If the center of G is of index n, then every conjugacy class has at most n elements.
GroupTheory.abelian groups and inner automorphisms?: A group G is abelian if and only if every inner automorphism is trivial.
GroupTheory.a subgroup, H, being characteristic in G: A subgroup H of a group G is called characteristic in G, denoted H char G, if every automorphism of G maps H to itself.
GroupTheory.characteristic subgroups and unique subg: If H is the unique subgroup of a given order, then H is characteristic in G.
GroupTheory.can we conclude about characteristic sub: A characteristic subgroup of a normal subgroup is a normal subgroup.
GroupTheory.characteristic subgroups of characterist: A characteristic subgroup of a characteristic subgroup is a characteristic subgroup.
GroupTheory.can we conclude about groups of order pq: If |G| = pqr, where p, q, and r are primes with p \lt q \lt r, then G has a normal Sylow subgroup of order either p, q, or r.
GroupTheory.the center of the Dihedral Group?: For n = 1 or n = 2, Z(D_n) = D_n       If n \ge 3, then Z(D_n) = 1
GroupTheory.Torsion subgroup of G always a subgroup: If G is abelian, then the Torsion subgroup of G is a subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.the Torsion subgroup of G?: The Torsion subgroup of G is {g \in G | |g| < \infty} (all elements of finite order)
GroupTheory.group generated by x equal to the group: \ltx\gt = \ltx^a\gt IF AND ONLY IF a = 1 or a = -1
GroupTheory.the subgroup generated by A?: Tge subgroup generated by A is the intersection of all subgroups containing A.      \ltA\gt = \capH, s.t. H \le G, and A \subseteq H.
GroupTheory.we decompose abelian groups?: Abelian groups A \cong F_A \oplus T_A, where F_A is the free part and T_A is the torsion part.
GroupTheory.we decompose the set of homomorphisms be: If A and B are two abelian groups, then       Hom(A, B) \cong Hom(F_A. F_B) \oplus Hom(F_A, T_B) \oplus Hom(T_A, F_B) \oplus Hom(T_A, T_B)
GroupTheory.HK a normal subgroup of G?: If H normalizes K then HK is a subgroup of G.
GroupTheory.the order of a subgroup and the index of: If a subgroup's order is relatively prime to the index of a normal subgroup , then the subgroup is contained in the normal subgroup.
GroupTheory.the ralationship between the order of a: If the order of a subgroup is relatively prime to its index, then its characteristic in G.
GroupTheory.a subgroup of prime index and other subg: If H \triangleleft G and [H : G] = p, then for all K \subseteq G, either       K \subseteq H, or       G = HK and [K : K \cap H] = p
GroupTheory.can we conclude about simple groups of o: If G is simple and of odd order, then G is isomorphic to \mathbb{Z}_p for some prime p.
GroupTheory.a solvable group?: G is solvable if there is a chain of subgroups       1 = G_0 \triangleleft G_1 \triangleleft ... \triangleleft G_s = G       such that G_{i+1}/G_i is abelian for i = 0, 1, ..., s-1
GroupTheory.a finite group G and the factors of G?: A finite group is solvable IF AND ONLY IF for every divisor n of G where gcd(n, |G|/n) = 1, G has a subgroup of order n.
GroupTheory.solvable groups and quotent groups?: If N and G / N are solvable, then so is G
GroupTheory.can we say about the order of the subgro: Every finite abelian group has a subgroup of order n for each positive divisor of its order.
GroupTheory.can we say about the subgroups whose ind: If G is finite and of order n and p is the smallest prime dividing |G|, then any subgroup of index p is normal in G.
GroupTheory.the center of a group and the size of it: If the center of G has index n, then every conjugacy class has at most n elements.
GroupTheory.the normalizer, the centralizer and homo: N_g(H) / C_g(H) is isomorphic to some subgroup of Aut(H).
